name: UAE Dirham
description: The UAE Dirham has been pegged to the US Dollar since 1997. The UAE has diversified its economy beyond oil into tourism, finance, and logistics.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1973, replacing the Bahraini dinar (in Abu Dhabi) and the Qatar and Dubai riyal. The UAE has become a global financial hub, particularly Dubai.

name: Afghan Afghani
description: The Afghan Afghani is the currency of Afghanistan. Multiple currencies including the US dollar and Pakistani rupee circulate in practice.
historicalNotes: The current afghani was introduced in 2002 after the fall of the Taliban, replacing the previous afghani at 1,000:1.

name: Albanian Lek
description: The Albanian Lek is named after Alexander the Great (Leka i Madh in Albanian).
historicalNotes: First introduced in 1926. After the fall of communism in 1991, Albania transitioned to a market economy and the lek became freely convertible.

name: Armenian Dram
description: The Armenian Dram is the currency of Armenia, one of the world's oldest civilizations.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1993 after Armenia's independence from the Soviet Union. Armenia was the first country to adopt Christianity as a state religion.

name: Netherlands Antillean Guilder
centralBank: Central Bank of Curaçao and Sint Maarten
description: The Netherlands Antillean Guilder continues to be used in Curaçao and Sint Maarten, both constituent countries of the Kingdom of the Netherlands.
historicalNotes: The guilder survived the dissolution of the Netherlands Antilles in 2010. Plans exist to replace it with a Caribbean guilder.

name: Angolan Kwanza
description: The Angolan Kwanza is named after the Kwanza River. Angola is Africa's second-largest oil producer.
historicalNotes: Angola has gone through several currency reforms. The current kwanza was introduced in 1999. The country's economy is heavily dependent on oil exports.

name: Argentine Peso
description: The Argentine Peso has experienced significant volatility and multiple devaluations throughout its history, reflecting Argentina's complex economic challenges.
historicalNotes: Argentina has changed its currency multiple times. The current peso was introduced in 1992, replacing the austral at a rate of 10,000:1. It was pegged to the US dollar until the 2001 economic crisis.

name: Australian Dollar
description: The Australian Dollar is the fifth most traded currency globally. Australia pioneered polymer banknotes, now used by over 50 countries.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1966, replacing the Australian pound. Australia was the first country to issue polymer banknotes in 1988, which are more durable and harder to counterfeit.

name: Aruban Florin
description: The Aruban Florin is the currency of Aruba, a constituent country of the Kingdom of the Netherlands in the Caribbean.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1986 when Aruba separated from the Netherlands Antilles, replacing the Netherlands Antillean guilder.

name: Azerbaijani Manat
description: The Azerbaijani Manat is the currency of Azerbaijan, a major oil and gas producer in the Caspian region.
historicalNotes: The 'new manat' was introduced in 2006, removing four zeros. Azerbaijan's economy is heavily dependent on hydrocarbon exports.

name: Bosnia and Herzegovina Convertible Mark
centralBank: Central Bank of Bosnia and Herzegovina
description: The Convertible Mark is pegged to the Euro at the same rate the Deutsche Mark was converted (1 EUR = 1.95583 KM).
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1998 under the Dayton Agreement. The currency board arrangement has provided monetary stability to the post-war economy.

name: Barbadian Dollar
description: The Barbadian Dollar has been pegged to the US Dollar at 2:1 since 1975. Barbados became a republic in 2021.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1973, replacing the East Caribbean dollar. Barbados is the easternmost Caribbean island.

name: Bangladeshi Taka
description: The Taka is the currency of Bangladesh, the world's second-largest garment exporter.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1972 after Bangladesh's independence from Pakistan. Bangladesh has achieved significant economic growth and poverty reduction since the 1990s.

name: Bulgarian Lev
description: The Bulgarian Lev has been pegged to the Euro (and previously the Deutsche Mark) through a currency board arrangement since 1997.
historicalNotes: The current lev was redenominated in 1999 at 1,000:1. Bulgaria joined the ERM II in 2020, a step toward eventual Euro adoption.

name: Bahraini Dinar
description: The Bahraini Dinar is one of the world's highest-valued currencies. Bahrain is a major financial center in the Gulf region.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1965, replacing the Gulf rupee. Bahrain was the first Gulf state to discover oil (1932) and the first to diversify into financial services.

name: Burundian Franc
centralBank: Bank of the Republic of Burundi
description: The Burundian Franc is the currency of one of the world's smallest and poorest countries.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1964, replacing the Congo-Burundi franc. Burundi is one of the most densely populated countries in Africa.

name: Bermudian Dollar
description: The Bermudian Dollar is pegged at par with the US Dollar. Bermuda is a British Overseas Territory and a major offshore financial center.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1970, replacing the Bermudian pound. Bermuda is home to many international insurance and reinsurance companies.

name: Brunei Dollar
description: The Brunei Dollar is interchangeable with the Singapore Dollar at par. Brunei is one of the world's wealthiest nations per capita due to oil and gas.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1967. The Currency Interchangeability Agreement with Singapore has been in place since 1967.

name: Bolivian Boliviano
description: The Boliviano is named after Simón Bolívar, the liberator of several South American nations. Bolivia has significant natural gas reserves.
historicalNotes: The current boliviano was introduced in 1987, replacing the peso boliviano at a rate of 1,000,000:1 following hyperinflation in the 1980s.

name: Brazilian Real
description: The Brazilian Real is the most traded currency in Latin America. Brazil's economy is the largest in South America and among the top ten globally.
historicalNotes: Brazil has had multiple currencies due to inflation. The real was introduced in 1994 as part of the Plano Real economic stabilization plan, which successfully curbed hyperinflation.

name: Bahamian Dollar
description: The Bahamian Dollar is pegged at par with the US Dollar. The Bahamas launched the world's first central bank digital currency, the Sand Dollar, in 2020.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1966, replacing the Bahamian pound at a rate of 1 dollar = 7 shillings.

name: Bhutanese Ngultrum
description: The Bhutanese Ngultrum is pegged at par with the Indian Rupee. Bhutan is famous for measuring Gross National Happiness.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1974. Bhutan was the last country in the world to introduce television, in 1999.

name: Botswana Pula
description: The Botswana Pula means 'rain' in Setswana — a precious commodity in this semi-arid country. Botswana is the world's leading diamond producer by value.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1976, replacing the South African rand. Botswana's transformation from one of the world's poorest countries to an upper-middle-income economy through diamond wealth and good governance is remarkable.

name: Belarusian Ruble
centralBank: National Bank of the Republic of Belarus
description: The Belarusian Ruble is the currency of Belarus, a landlocked country in Eastern Europe.
historicalNotes: The current 'new' ruble was introduced in 2016, removing four zeros. Belarus has experienced multiple currency crises and redenominations since independence.

name: Belize Dollar
description: The Belize Dollar has been pegged to the US Dollar at a rate of 2:1 since 1978. Belize is the only English-speaking country in Central America.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1973 when British Honduras was renamed Belize, replacing the British Honduras dollar at par.

name: Canadian Dollar
description: The Canadian Dollar, often called the 'loonie' after the common loon depicted on its one-dollar coin, is the fifth most held reserve currency in the world.
historicalNotes: Canada adopted the dollar in 1858, replacing the Canadian pound. The currency was pegged to the US dollar from 1962 to 1970, after which it returned to a floating exchange rate.

name: Congolese Franc
description: The Congolese Franc is the currency of the DRC, which holds vast mineral wealth including cobalt essential for batteries.
historicalNotes: The franc was reintroduced in 1997, replacing the new zaïre when the country was renamed from Zaïre to the Democratic Republic of the Congo.

name: Swiss Franc
description: The Swiss Franc is considered one of the world's strongest and most stable currencies, and serves as a global safe-haven currency.
historicalNotes: The franc was introduced in 1850, unifying the currencies of the Swiss cantons. Switzerland has maintained a tradition of monetary stability and banking secrecy, though banking transparency has increased in recent decades.

name: Chilean Peso
description: The Chilean Peso is the currency of one of South America's most stable and open economies, known for its copper exports.
historicalNotes: The current peso was reintroduced in 1975, replacing the escudo at a rate of 1,000:1. Chile pioneered inflation-targeting monetary policy in Latin America.

name: Chinese Yuan Renminbi
description: The Yuan Renminbi ('people's currency') is the currency of the world's second-largest economy. China has been internationalizing the yuan as part of its economic strategy.
historicalNotes: The renminbi was introduced in 1949 by the People's Republic of China. It was tightly pegged to the US dollar until 2005, when China moved to a managed float. The yuan was added to the IMF's Special Drawing Rights basket in 2016.

name: Colombian Peso
description: The Colombian Peso is the currency of Colombia, the fourth-largest economy in Latin America, known for coffee and emerald exports.
historicalNotes: The peso has been Colombia's currency since 1837, replacing the real. Colombia adopted inflation targeting in 1999 and has maintained relative monetary stability.

name: Costa Rican Colón
description: The Colón is named after Christopher Columbus (Cristóbal Colón in Spanish). Costa Rica is known for its stable democracy and environmental conservation.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1896, replacing the peso. Costa Rica abolished its military in 1948, redirecting resources to education and healthcare.

name: Cuban Peso
description: The Cuban Peso is the official currency of Cuba. For decades Cuba operated a dual currency system with the convertible peso (CUC).
historicalNotes: Cuba unified its dual currency system in January 2021, eliminating the convertible peso (CUC) and leaving only the Cuban peso (CUP).

name: Cape Verdean Escudo
description: The Cape Verdean Escudo is pegged to the Euro through a special partnership agreement with Portugal.
historicalNotes: The escudo was adopted in 1914. Cape Verde's special peg to the Euro reflects its historical ties to Portugal and its strategic location in the mid-Atlantic.

name: Czech Koruna
description: The Czech Koruna is the currency of one of Europe's most industrialized economies, known for its automotive and engineering sectors.
historicalNotes: Introduced on February 8, 1993, when Czechoslovakia peacefully dissolved. It replaced the Czechoslovak koruna.

name: Djiboutian Franc
description: The Djiboutian Franc has been pegged to the US Dollar since 1973. Djibouti hosts military bases for several countries due to its strategic location.
historicalNotes: Djibouti gained independence from France in 1977 and maintained the franc.

name: Danish Krone
description: The Danish Krone is pegged to the Euro through the ERM II mechanism. Denmark rejected Euro adoption in a 2000 referendum.
historicalNotes: The krone was introduced in 1873 as part of the Scandinavian Monetary Union. Denmark has maintained a fixed exchange rate policy, first to the Deutsche Mark and now to the Euro.

name: Dominican Peso
centralBank: Central Bank of the Dominican Republic
description: The Dominican Peso is the currency of the Dominican Republic, the largest Caribbean economy by GDP.
historicalNotes: The peso was introduced in 1844 when the Dominican Republic gained independence. The country briefly used the US dollar from 1905 to 1947.

name: Algerian Dinar
description: The Algerian Dinar is the currency of Africa's largest country by area, a major natural gas and oil exporter.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1964, replacing the Algerian franc two years after independence from France.

name: Egyptian Pound
description: The Egyptian Pound is the currency of Egypt, home to the Suez Canal and Africa's third-largest economy.
historicalNotes: Egypt adopted the pound in 1834. The currency was pegged to the British pound until 1962, then to the US dollar. Egypt floated the pound in 2016 as part of an IMF reform program.

name: Eritrean Nakfa
description: The Eritrean Nakfa is named after the town of Nakfa, symbolic of Eritrea's independence struggle.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1997, replacing the Ethiopian birr, four years after Eritrea gained independence from Ethiopia.

name: Ethiopian Birr
description: The Ethiopian Birr is the currency of Africa's second most populous country and one of its fastest-growing economies.
historicalNotes: Ethiopia is one of only two African countries never colonized. The birr was introduced in 1945, replacing the East African shilling used during the Italian occupation.

name: Euro
description: The Euro is the official currency of 20 EU member states (the Eurozone) and is the second most traded currency globally after the US Dollar. It is also used by several European microstates and overseas territories.
historicalNotes: The Euro was introduced as an accounting currency in 1999 and as physical coins and banknotes on January 1, 2002. It replaced national currencies like the Deutsche Mark, French Franc, Italian Lira, and Spanish Peseta. Croatia became the 20th Eurozone member in 2023.

name: Fijian Dollar
description: The Fijian Dollar is the currency of Fiji, the tourism and economic hub of the South Pacific.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1969, replacing the Fijian pound. Fiji is the most developed Pacific Island economy.

name: Falkland Islands Pound
description: The Falkland Islands Pound is pegged at par with the British Pound Sterling. The islands are a British Overseas Territory in the South Atlantic.
historicalNotes: The Falklands have used their own currency since 1899. The 1982 Falklands War between the UK and Argentina made the islands internationally prominent.

name: Pound Sterling
description: The Pound Sterling is the world's oldest currency still in use and the fourth most traded currency in forex markets.
historicalNotes: Sterling has been in use since Anglo-Saxon times. The Bank of England was established in 1694. Britain left the gold standard in 1931. Decimalization occurred in 1971, replacing the old pounds/shillings/pence system.

name: Georgian Lari
description: The Lari means 'hoard' or 'property' in Georgian. Georgia lies at the crossroads of Europe and Asia.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1995, replacing the provisional coupon currency used after independence from the Soviet Union.

name: Ghanaian Cedi
description: The Ghanaian Cedi (meaning 'cowrie shell') is the currency of Ghana, the world's second-largest cocoa producer.
historicalNotes: The new cedi was introduced in 2007, removing four zeros. Ghana was the first sub-Saharan African country to gain independence (1957).

name: Gibraltar Pound
description: The Gibraltar Pound is pegged at par with the British Pound Sterling. Gibraltar is a British Overseas Territory at the southern tip of the Iberian Peninsula.
historicalNotes: Gibraltar has issued its own banknotes and coins since 1898. The territory is strategically located at the entrance to the Mediterranean Sea.

name: Gambian Dalasi
description: The Gambian Dalasi is the currency of The Gambia, Africa's smallest mainland country.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1971, replacing the Gambian pound. The Gambia is entirely surrounded by Senegal except for its Atlantic coastline.

name: Guinean Franc
centralBank: Central Bank of the Republic of Guinea
description: The Guinean Franc is the currency of Guinea, which holds the world's largest reserves of bauxite (aluminum ore).
historicalNotes: Reintroduced in 1985, replacing the Guinean syli. Guinea was the first French African colony to gain independence in 1958.

name: Guatemalan Quetzal
description: The Quetzal is named after the national bird of Guatemala, the resplendent quetzal, which was sacred to the ancient Maya.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1925, replacing the peso. In Mayan civilization, quetzal feathers were used as currency, making this one of the few modern currencies with pre-Columbian monetary roots.

name: Guyanese Dollar
description: The Guyanese Dollar is the currency of the only English-speaking country in South America. Recent oil discoveries have transformed Guyana's economic outlook.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1966 when Guyana gained independence from Britain, replacing the British Guiana dollar at par.

name: Hong Kong Dollar
description: The Hong Kong Dollar is pegged to the US Dollar through a linked exchange rate system. Hong Kong is a major international financial center.
historicalNotes: The dollar has been used since 1863. The linked exchange rate system with the US dollar was established in 1983 during a confidence crisis. Three commercial banks (HSBC, Standard Chartered, Bank of China) issue banknotes.

name: Honduran Lempira
description: The Lempira is named after a 16th-century indigenous leader who resisted Spanish conquest.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1931, replacing the peso at a rate of 1:1. Honduras has maintained relatively stable monetary policy compared to some regional peers.

name: Haitian Gourde
centralBank: Bank of the Republic of Haiti
description: The Gourde is the currency of Haiti, the first independent nation of Latin America and the Caribbean.
historicalNotes: The gourde was introduced in 1813, making it one of the oldest currencies in the Americas. It has gone through multiple devaluations.

name: Hungarian Forint
description: The Hungarian Forint is named after the gold coins of Florence (fiorino). Hungary has one of the most open economies in the EU.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1946 to replace the pengő after the worst hyperinflation in history. Hungary is an EU member but has not set a target date for Euro adoption.

name: Indonesian Rupiah
description: The Indonesian Rupiah is the currency of the world's largest archipelago nation and Southeast Asia's largest economy.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1946 during Indonesia's independence struggle. The rupiah lost about 80% of its value during the 1997 Asian Financial Crisis.

name: Israeli New Shekel
description: The Israeli New Shekel is the currency of Israel, a global leader in technology and innovation.
historicalNotes: The 'new shekel' was introduced in 1985, replacing the old shekel at 1,000:1 after a period of hyperinflation. The shekel name has ancient origins from biblical times.

name: Indian Rupee
description: The Indian Rupee is the currency of the world's most populous country and fifth-largest economy. India is a major player in IT services and pharmaceuticals.
historicalNotes: The rupee has ancient origins, with the name derived from the Sanskrit 'rūpya' (silver coin). Modern decimalization occurred in 1957. The ₹ symbol was adopted in 2010. India's 2016 demonetization of ₹500 and ₹1000 notes was one of the largest monetary experiments in history.

name: Iraqi Dinar
description: The Iraqi Dinar is the currency of Iraq, which holds the world's fifth-largest proven oil reserves.
historicalNotes: New banknotes were introduced in 2003 after the fall of Saddam Hussein, unifying the 'Swiss dinar' used in Kurdistan with the dinar used in the rest of Iraq.

name: Iranian Rial
description: The Iranian Rial is one of the world's lowest-valued currencies. Iranians commonly use the 'toman' (1 toman = 10 rials) in daily transactions.
historicalNotes: The rial has been used since 1798. International sanctions have significantly affected the currency's value since 2012.

name: Icelandic Króna
description: The Icelandic Króna is the currency of one of the world's smallest economies to have its own independent currency and monetary policy.
historicalNotes: Iceland adopted its own króna in 1918 when it gained sovereignty from Denmark. The 2008 financial crisis severely affected the currency, which lost about half its value.

name: Jamaican Dollar
description: The Jamaican Dollar is the currency of the Caribbean island nation known for its tourism, music, and athletics.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1969, replacing the Jamaican pound. The dollar was initially pegged to the US dollar but has since moved to a managed float.

name: Jordanian Dinar
description: The Jordanian Dinar is one of the highest-valued currencies in the world, pegged to the US Dollar since 1995.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1950, replacing the Palestinian pound. Jordan's economy relies on services, tourism, and remittances.

name: Japanese Yen
description: The Japanese Yen is the third most traded currency globally and a major reserve currency. Japan has the world's third-largest economy.
historicalNotes: The yen was introduced in 1871 during the Meiji era modernization. After World War II, the yen was pegged to the US dollar at 360:1 until 1971. Japan's 'lost decades' of deflation from the 1990s shaped modern monetary policy globally.

name: Kenyan Shilling
description: The Kenyan Shilling is the currency of East Africa's largest economy, known for its mobile money innovation (M-Pesa).
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1966, replacing the East African shilling. Kenya's M-Pesa mobile payment system revolutionized financial inclusion globally.

name: Kyrgyzstani Som
centralBank: National Bank of the Kyrgyz Republic
description: The Kyrgyzstani Som means 'pure' in Kyrgyz, referring to pure gold.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1993 after independence from the Soviet Union. Kyrgyzstan was one of the first Central Asian countries to adopt market-oriented reforms.

name: Cambodian Riel
description: The Cambodian Riel circulates alongside the US Dollar, which dominates larger transactions. Cambodia's economy has grown rapidly through garment exports and tourism.
historicalNotes: The riel was reintroduced in 1980 after the Khmer Rouge era. During the Pol Pot regime (1975-1979), money was abolished entirely.

name: Comorian Franc
description: The Comorian Franc is pegged to the Euro through a monetary cooperation agreement with France.
historicalNotes: Comoros gained independence from France in 1975. The franc maintains a fixed exchange rate with the Euro.

name: North Korean Won
centralBank: Central Bank of the Democratic People's Republic of Korea
description: The North Korean Won is a non-convertible currency used domestically. Foreign exchange is tightly controlled by the state.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1947. North Korea redenominated its currency in 2009, removing two zeros, causing economic disruption. The black market exchange rate differs vastly from official rates.

name: South Korean Won
description: The South Korean Won is the currency of the world's 10th-largest economy, known for technology exports from companies like Samsung and Hyundai.
historicalNotes: The current won was introduced in 1962. South Korea's rapid economic development from the 1960s ('Miracle on the Han River') transformed it from one of the world's poorest countries to a high-income economy.

name: Kuwaiti Dinar
description: The Kuwaiti Dinar is the world's highest-valued currency unit. Kuwait holds approximately 7% of the world's proven oil reserves.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1961, replacing the Gulf rupee. During the 1990 Iraqi invasion, the dinar was briefly replaced by the Iraqi dinar but restored after liberation.

name: Cayman Islands Dollar
description: The Cayman Islands Dollar is pegged to the US Dollar. The Cayman Islands are one of the world's largest offshore financial centers.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1972, replacing the Jamaican dollar. The Cayman Islands have no direct taxation.

name: Kazakhstani Tenge
description: The Kazakhstani Tenge is the currency of Central Asia's largest economy, rich in oil and mineral resources.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1993 after Kazakhstan's independence from the Soviet Union. The currency was allowed to float freely in 2015 after years of managed exchange rates.

name: Lao Kip
description: The Lao Kip is the currency of Laos, a landlocked Southeast Asian country. In practice, Thai baht and US dollars are widely used alongside the kip.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1952. The kip has experienced significant inflation, making it one of the world's lowest-valued currency units.

name: Lebanese Pound
description: The Lebanese Pound has experienced severe devaluation since the 2019 financial crisis, losing over 90% of its value.
historicalNotes: Lebanon maintained a stable peg to the US dollar from 1997 to 2019. The subsequent financial crisis created multiple exchange rates and wiped out savings.

name: Sri Lankan Rupee
description: The Sri Lankan Rupee is the currency of the island nation known for tea, spices, and textiles.
historicalNotes: Sri Lanka adopted its own rupee after independence from Britain in 1948. The country experienced a severe economic crisis in 2022 that significantly affected the currency.

name: Liberian Dollar
description: The Liberian Dollar circulates alongside the US Dollar. Liberia was founded by freed American slaves in the 19th century.
historicalNotes: Liberia used the US dollar until 1943 when it introduced its own dollar. Both currencies remain legal tender.

name: Lesotho Loti
description: The Lesotho Loti (plural: maloti) is pegged at par with the South African Rand. Lesotho is entirely surrounded by South Africa.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1980. Lesotho is the only country in the world that lies entirely above 1,000 meters in elevation.

name: Libyan Dinar
description: The Libyan Dinar is the currency of Libya, which holds Africa's largest proven oil reserves.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1971, replacing the Libyan pound. Political instability since 2011 has created competing monetary authorities.

name: Moroccan Dirham
description: The Moroccan Dirham is pegged to a basket dominated by the Euro, reflecting Morocco's strong trade ties with Europe.
historicalNotes: The dirham was reintroduced in 1960, replacing the franc. Morocco has gradually liberalized its exchange rate since 2018.

name: Moldovan Leu
description: The Moldovan Leu shares its name ('lion') with the Romanian leu, reflecting the countries' shared linguistic and cultural heritage.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1993 after Moldova gained independence from the Soviet Union. Moldova is the poorest country in Europe by GDP per capita.

name: Malagasy Ariary
description: The Malagasy Ariary is one of only two circulating currencies not subdivided into hundredths — it is subdivided into five iraimbilanja.
historicalNotes: The ariary replaced the Malagasy franc in 2005. Madagascar is the world's fourth-largest island and home to unique biodiversity.

name: Macedonian Denar
description: The Macedonian Denar is named after the ancient Roman denarius coin. North Macedonia is a candidate for EU membership.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1992 when North Macedonia declared independence from Yugoslavia.

name: Myanmar Kyat
description: The Myanmar Kyat is the currency of Myanmar (Burma), a country rich in natural resources including jade, rubies, and natural gas.
historicalNotes: The kyat was introduced in 1952. Myanmar maintained a dual exchange rate system for decades, with a large gap between official and black market rates. Market-based exchange rate was adopted in 2012.

name: Mongolian Tögrög
description: The Mongolian Tögrög is the currency of Mongolia, known for its vast steppes and mining sector.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1925, replacing the Mongolian dollar. Mongolia's economy is heavily dependent on copper and gold mining.

name: Macanese Pataca
description: The Macanese Pataca is pegged to the Hong Kong Dollar. Macau is the world's largest gambling center by revenue.
historicalNotes: The pataca was introduced during Portuguese administration. Macau returned to Chinese sovereignty in 1999 but maintains its own currency as a Special Administrative Region.

name: Mauritanian Ouguiya
description: The Ouguiya was historically one of only two currencies not subdivided into hundredths (it used fifths). A new ouguiya was introduced in 2018.
historicalNotes: The new ouguiya was introduced in 2018, replacing the old ouguiya at 10:1. The name comes from the Arabic word for 'ounce'.

name: Mauritian Rupee
description: The Mauritian Rupee is the currency of Mauritius, one of Africa's most prosperous and stable economies.
historicalNotes: Mauritius has used the rupee since 1877, reflecting its historical connections to India and British colonial rule.

name: Maldivian Rufiyaa
description: The Maldivian Rufiyaa is the currency of the Maldives, a low-lying island nation threatened by rising sea levels.
historicalNotes: The rufiyaa has been used since 1947. The Maldives is the world's lowest-lying country, with an average elevation of just 1.5 meters.

name: Malawian Kwacha
description: The Malawian Kwacha also means 'dawn' in the local Chewa language.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1971, replacing the Malawian pound. Malawi's economy is heavily dependent on agriculture, particularly tobacco.

name: Mexican Peso
description: The Mexican Peso is one of the most traded currencies in the Americas and among emerging market currencies globally. Mexico was one of the first countries to use the dollar sign '$'.
historicalNotes: The peso has its origins in the Spanish real de a ocho (piece of eight). After hyperinflation in the 1980s, the 'nuevo peso' was introduced in 1993, dropping three zeros. It reverted to simply 'peso' in 1996.

name: Malaysian Ringgit
description: The Malaysian Ringgit means 'jagged' in Malay, referring to the jagged edges of Spanish silver dollars that once circulated in the region.
historicalNotes: The ringgit replaced the Malaya and British Borneo dollar in 1967. During the 1997 Asian Financial Crisis, Malaysia controversially pegged the ringgit to the US dollar and imposed capital controls.

name: Mozambican Metical
description: The Mozambican Metical is named after a gold coin weight used in Arab trade along the East African coast.
historicalNotes: The 'new metical' was introduced in 2006, removing three zeros. Mozambique was a Portuguese colony until 1975.

name: Namibian Dollar
description: The Namibian Dollar is pegged at par with the South African Rand. Both currencies are legal tender in Namibia.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1993, three years after Namibia gained independence from South Africa. The country is known for its diamond mining and tourism.

name: Nigerian Naira
description: The Nigerian Naira is the currency of Africa's largest economy and most populous country. Nigeria is a major oil producer.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1973, replacing the Nigerian pound. Nigeria has maintained various exchange rate regimes and multiple rates over the decades.

name: Nicaraguan Córdoba
description: The Córdoba is named after the founder of Nicaragua, Francisco Hernández de Córdoba.
historicalNotes: The current córdoba oro was introduced in 1990, replacing the old córdoba at 5,000,000:1 after hyperinflation during the Sandinista era.

name: Norwegian Krone
description: The Norwegian Krone is backed by one of the world's largest sovereign wealth funds, the Government Pension Fund Global, funded by petroleum revenues.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1875 when Norway joined the Scandinavian Monetary Union. Norway's massive oil wealth has made the krone a petrocurrency.

name: Nepalese Rupee
description: The Nepalese Rupee is pegged to the Indian Rupee at 1.6 NPR per 1 INR, reflecting the close economic ties between the two countries.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1932, replacing the Nepalese mohar. Nepal is the only country with a non-rectangular national flag.

name: New Zealand Dollar
description: The New Zealand Dollar, nicknamed the 'Kiwi', is the currency of New Zealand. The country pioneered inflation targeting, now adopted by dozens of central banks.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1967, replacing the New Zealand pound. New Zealand was the first country to adopt formal inflation targeting in 1990.

name: Omani Rial
description: The Omani Rial is the third-highest valued currency in the world. Oman is diversifying its economy through its Vision 2040 plan.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1970, replacing the Gulf rupee and Saudi riyal that had circulated previously.

name: Panamanian Balboa
description: The Panamanian Balboa is pegged at par with the US Dollar and circulates alongside it. Panama has no central bank in the traditional sense and uses the US dollar for paper currency.
historicalNotes: Named after Spanish explorer Vasco Núñez de Balboa, who discovered the Pacific Ocean. Panama only mints coins; all paper currency is US dollars.

name: Peruvian Sol
description: The Peruvian Sol is named after the Latin word for sun. Peru's economy is one of the fastest-growing in Latin America.
historicalNotes: The 'nuevo sol' was introduced in 1991 to replace the inti, which had suffered hyperinflation. It was renamed simply 'sol' in 2015.

name: Papua New Guinean Kina
description: The Kina is named after a type of shell traditionally used as currency in Papua New Guinea, the world's most linguistically diverse country.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1975 when Papua New Guinea gained independence from Australia. The country has over 800 languages.

name: Philippine Peso
description: The Philippine Peso is the currency of the Philippines, an archipelago of over 7,000 islands. Remittances from overseas Filipino workers are a major source of foreign exchange.
historicalNotes: The peso has been used since Spanish colonial times. The Philippines was one of the first Asian countries to adopt a decimal currency system.

name: Pakistani Rupee
description: The Pakistani Rupee is the currency of the world's fifth most populous country.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1948 after Pakistan's independence from British India. The rupee has experienced significant devaluation over the decades.

name: Polish Złoty
description: The Polish Złoty is the currency of Central Europe's largest economy. The name means 'golden' in Polish.
historicalNotes: The current złoty was introduced in 1995, replacing the old złoty at 10,000:1. Poland is an EU member but has delayed Euro adoption.

name: Paraguayan Guaraní
description: The Guaraní is named after the indigenous Guaraní people and is one of the lower-valued currencies in the Americas.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1943, replacing the peso at par. Paraguay has maintained this currency longer than most South American nations, though it has experienced significant inflation.

name: Qatari Riyal
description: The Qatari Riyal is pegged to the US Dollar. Qatar has the world's highest GDP per capita, driven by natural gas exports.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1973, replacing the Qatar and Dubai riyal after the UAE was formed.

name: Romanian Leu
description: The Romanian Leu (plural: lei) means 'lion' in Romanian. Romania has one of the fastest-growing economies in the EU.
historicalNotes: The 'new leu' was introduced in 2005, removing four zeros from the old leu. Romania plans to adopt the Euro but has not met all convergence criteria.

name: Serbian Dinar
description: The Serbian Dinar is the currency of Serbia, the largest economy in the Western Balkans.
historicalNotes: The modern dinar was introduced in 2003, following Serbia and Montenegro's restructuring. Serbia is a candidate for EU membership.

name: Russian Ruble
description: The Russian Ruble is one of the world's oldest currencies. Russia possesses vast natural resources including oil, gas, and minerals.
historicalNotes: The ruble dates back to the 13th century. The current ruble was redenominated in 1998, removing three zeros. International sanctions since 2022 have significantly impacted the currency's global convertibility.

name: Rwandan Franc
description: The Rwandan Franc is the currency of Rwanda, which has achieved remarkable economic growth and is known as the 'Singapore of Africa'.
historicalNotes: Rwanda has maintained the franc since independence in 1962. The country's post-genocide recovery and economic transformation has been widely recognized.

name: Saudi Riyal
description: The Saudi Riyal has been pegged to the US Dollar at 3.75 since 1986. Saudi Arabia is the world's largest oil exporter.
historicalNotes: The riyal was introduced in its modern form in 1925. The currency's stability is underpinned by Saudi Arabia's vast oil wealth and foreign exchange reserves.

name: Solomon Islands Dollar
description: The Solomon Islands Dollar is the currency of the Solomon Islands, a Melanesian archipelago of over 900 islands.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1977, replacing the Australian dollar two years after independence.

name: Seychellois Rupee
description: The Seychellois Rupee is the currency of the Seychelles, an archipelago of 115 islands in the Indian Ocean.
historicalNotes: The Seychelles used the Mauritian rupee until 1914 when it introduced its own. The country floated its currency in 2008.

name: Sudanese Pound
description: The Sudanese Pound has experienced significant devaluation. Sudan's economy has been affected by conflict and international sanctions.
historicalNotes: The current pound was introduced in 2007, replacing the Sudanese dinar. South Sudan's secession in 2011 cost Sudan significant oil revenues.

name: Swedish Krona
description: The Swedish Krona is issued by the world's oldest central bank, Sveriges Riksbank (est. 1668). Sweden is one of the world's most cashless societies.
historicalNotes: The krona was introduced in 1873 when Sweden joined the Scandinavian Monetary Union. Sweden is an EU member but has opted out of the Eurozone.

name: Singapore Dollar
description: The Singapore Dollar is managed through a unique exchange rate-based monetary policy rather than interest rate targeting.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1967 after Singapore's separation from Malaysia. Singapore and Brunei maintain a Currency Interchangeability Agreement, accepting each other's currency at par.

name: Sierra Leonean Leone
description: The Sierra Leonean Leone is the currency of Sierra Leone, known for its diamond mining. A new leone was introduced in 2022.
historicalNotes: The new leone (SLE) was introduced in 2022, replacing the old leone at 1,000:1. Sierra Leone was founded as a settlement for freed slaves.

name: Somali Shilling
description: The Somali Shilling has been affected by decades of civil conflict. Multiple currencies including US dollars circulate in different regions.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1962. During the civil war, counterfeiting and the absence of central banking led to massive inflation.

name: Surinamese Dollar
description: The Surinamese Dollar is the currency of the smallest sovereign state in South America.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 2004, replacing the Surinamese guilder at a rate of 1,000:1. Suriname was formerly a Dutch colony.

name: South Sudanese Pound
description: The South Sudanese Pound is the currency of the world's youngest nation, which gained independence in 2011.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 2011 when South Sudan gained independence from Sudan. The currency has suffered severe inflation due to civil war and oil production disruptions.

name: São Tomé and Príncipe Dobra
centralBank: Central Bank of São Tomé and Príncipe
description: The Dobra is the currency of São Tomé and Príncipe, one of Africa's smallest countries, located in the Gulf of Guinea.
historicalNotes: The 'new dobra' was introduced in 2018, removing three zeros. The country pegged its currency to the Euro in 2010.

name: Syrian Pound
description: The Syrian Pound has been severely affected by the civil war that began in 2011, with massive devaluation and economic disruption.
historicalNotes: The pound has been Syria's currency since 1919. The civil war caused the currency to lose over 99% of its pre-war value.

name: Eswatini Lilangeni
description: The Lilangeni is pegged at par with the South African Rand and both currencies circulate in Eswatini (formerly Swaziland).
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1974, replacing the South African rand as the primary currency. Eswatini is Africa's last absolute monarchy.

name: Thai Baht
description: The Thai Baht is the currency of Southeast Asia's second-largest economy. Thailand is a major tourism destination and auto manufacturing hub.
historicalNotes: The baht has been used since the Sukhothai Kingdom (13th century). Thailand was the epicenter of the 1997 Asian Financial Crisis when it was forced to float the baht, triggering a regional economic meltdown.

name: Tajikistani Somoni
description: The Somoni is named after Ismoil Somoni, the 9th-century founder of the Samanid dynasty.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 2000, replacing the Tajikistani ruble at 1,000:1. Tajikistan is heavily dependent on remittances from migrant workers in Russia.

name: Turkmenistani Manat
description: The Turkmenistani Manat is the currency of one of the world's most closed economies, rich in natural gas reserves.
historicalNotes: The current 'new manat' was introduced in 2009, replacing the old manat at 5,000:1.

name: Tunisian Dinar
description: The Tunisian Dinar is the currency of Tunisia, birthplace of the Arab Spring.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1958, replacing the Tunisian franc after independence from France.

name: Tongan Paʻanga
description: The Tongan Paʻanga is the currency of the Kingdom of Tonga, the Pacific's last remaining monarchy.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1967, replacing the Tongan pound. Tonga was never formally colonized.

name: Turkish Lira
centralBank: Central Bank of the Republic of Türkiye
description: The Turkish Lira is the currency of a G20 nation that bridges Europe and Asia. Turkey has one of the world's largest economies.
historicalNotes: The 'new Turkish lira' was introduced in 2005, removing six zeros from the old lira. It was renamed back to 'Turkish lira' in 2009. The currency has experienced significant depreciation since 2018.

name: Trinidad and Tobago Dollar
centralBank: Central Bank of Trinidad and Tobago
description: The Trinidad and Tobago Dollar is the currency of the twin-island Caribbean nation known for its petroleum and natural gas industries.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1964, replacing the British West Indies dollar. Trinidad and Tobago is the wealthiest Caribbean nation by GDP per capita.

name: New Taiwan Dollar
centralBank: Central Bank of the Republic of China (Taiwan)
description: The New Taiwan Dollar is the currency of Taiwan, a global leader in semiconductor manufacturing.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1949, replacing the old Taiwan dollar at 40,000:1 after hyperinflation during the Chinese Civil War.

name: Tanzanian Shilling
description: The Tanzanian Shilling is the currency of the United Republic of Tanzania, home to Kilimanjaro and the Serengeti.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1966, replacing the East African shilling. Tanzania was formed from the union of Tanganyika and Zanzibar in 1964.

name: Ukrainian Hryvnia
description: The Hryvnia is named after a medieval unit of weight used for gold and silver. Ukraine is one of the world's largest grain exporters.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1996, replacing the karbovanets. The currency has faced significant challenges during the conflict with Russia beginning in 2022.

name: Ugandan Shilling
description: The Ugandan Shilling is the currency of Uganda, known as the 'Pearl of Africa' for its diverse landscapes and wildlife.
historicalNotes: The current shilling was introduced in 1987, replacing the old shilling at 100:1.

name: United States Dollar
description: The US Dollar is the world's primary reserve currency and the most widely traded currency in foreign exchange markets. It serves as the official currency of the United States and several other countries, and is accepted as a de facto currency in many more.
historicalNotes: The dollar was established by the Coinage Act of 1792. It became the world's dominant reserve currency after the Bretton Woods Agreement of 1944. The Nixon Shock of 1971 ended the dollar's convertibility to gold, transitioning to a free-floating fiat currency.

name: Uruguayan Peso
description: The Uruguayan Peso is the currency of one of South America's most developed and stable democracies.
historicalNotes: The current peso was introduced in 1993, replacing the nuevo peso at a rate of 1,000:1. Uruguay has a strong banking tradition and is sometimes called the 'Switzerland of South America'.

name: Uzbekistani Sum
description: The Uzbekistani Sum is the currency of Central Asia's most populous country, located along the ancient Silk Road.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1994, replacing the Soviet ruble. Uzbekistan unified its official and black market exchange rates in 2017 as part of economic reforms.

name: Venezuelan Bolívar
description: The Venezuelan Bolívar Soberano is named after independence leader Simón Bolívar. Venezuela holds the world's largest proven oil reserves.
historicalNotes: Venezuela has redenominated its currency multiple times due to hyperinflation. The bolívar soberano was introduced in 2018, removing five zeros. A digital bolívar removed six more zeros in 2021.

name: Vietnamese Đồng
description: The Vietnamese Đồng is one of the world's lowest-valued currency units. Vietnam has become a major manufacturing hub and one of Asia's fastest-growing economies.
historicalNotes: The current đồng was unified after North and South Vietnam merged in 1978. Vietnam's Đổi Mới economic reforms since 1986 transformed it from a centrally planned to a market-oriented economy.

name: Vanuatu Vatu
description: The Vanuatu Vatu has no subdivision — it is one of the few currencies not divided into smaller units.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1981, a year after Vanuatu gained independence from joint British-French administration. Vanuatu is the only country in the world to have been governed by two colonial powers simultaneously.

name: Samoan Tālā
description: The Samoan Tālā is the currency of Samoa, the first Pacific Island nation to gain independence.
historicalNotes: Introduced in 1967. In 2011, Samoa moved to the west side of the International Date Line, skipping December 30 entirely.

name: Central African CFA Franc
description: The Central African CFA Franc is shared by six Central African nations. Like its West African counterpart, it is pegged to the Euro.
historicalNotes: Created in 1945 alongside the West African CFA franc. Despite having the same value, the two CFA francs are not interchangeable. The monetary arrangement has colonial origins.

name: East Caribbean Dollar
description: The East Caribbean Dollar is a shared currency used by six independent nations and two British territories in the Eastern Caribbean. It has been pegged to the US Dollar at 2.70:1 since 1976.
historicalNotes: Established in 1965, replacing the British West Indies dollar. The Eastern Caribbean Central Bank, based in Saint Kitts, manages monetary policy for all member states.

name: West African CFA Franc
centralBank: Central Bank of West African States (BCEAO)
description: The West African CFA Franc is shared by eight West African nations and is guaranteed by the French Treasury at a fixed rate to the Euro.
historicalNotes: Created in 1945 as the 'Franc of the French Colonies of Africa'. The CFA arrangement has been controversial, with critics arguing it limits monetary sovereignty. Plans for a new currency called the 'Eco' have been discussed.

name: CFP Franc
description: The CFP Franc is used in French overseas territories in the Pacific. It is pegged to the Euro through a fixed exchange rate.
historicalNotes: Created in 1945 as the 'Franc of the French Pacific Colonies'. Like the CFA franc in Africa, it maintains a fixed rate to the Euro guaranteed by the French Treasury.

name: Yemeni Rial
description: The Yemeni Rial is the currency of Yemen. The ongoing conflict has severely impacted the currency's value and stability.
historicalNotes: North and South Yemen each had their own currencies until unification in 1990, when the rial became the unified currency.

name: South African Rand
description: The South African Rand is Africa's most traded currency and also serves as legal tender in Eswatini, Lesotho, and Namibia through the Common Monetary Area.
historicalNotes: Named after the Witwatersrand ('Ridge of White Waters'), the area where most of South Africa's gold deposits were found. Introduced in 1961 when South Africa became a republic.

name: Zambian Kwacha
description: The Zambian Kwacha means 'dawn' in the Bemba language, symbolizing independence. Zambia is one of Africa's largest copper producers.
historicalNotes: The current kwacha was introduced in 2013, removing three zeros from the old kwacha.

name: Zimbabwe Gold (ZiG)
description: Zimbabwe introduced the gold-backed ZiG currency in 2024 after years of hyperinflation destroyed previous currencies. The US dollar remains widely used.
historicalNotes: Zimbabwe experienced the world's second-worst hyperinflation in 2008, with prices doubling every 24 hours. After abandoning the Zimbabwe dollar and using foreign currencies, the ZiG was introduced in 2024.
